text: Laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy for SMA syndrome presented by the VCU Minimally Invasive Surgery Center. The patient is a fifty-eight year old female who presented with nausea, vomiting, and vague abdominal discomfort after significant weight loss. She had recently lost her job and had chronic anxiety disorder contributing to her weight loss. Her physical exam is unremarkable. She is slim and had a non-tender abdomen. Her abdomen is flat and has no surgical scars.

An EGD demonstrated a distended stomach and proximal duodenum without neoplasia or mucosal abnormality. A CT scan confirmed these findings. Here is a representative cut of her CT scan illustrating her markedly dilated stomach. Additional slides reveal a narrow duodenum at the level of the aero-mesenteric angle. Her diagnosis is superior mesenteric artery syndrome. As the patient loses weight, there is a loss of the mesenteric fat pack. The outer mesenteric angle is sharper and results in an occluded duodenum.

The surgeon prepares to bypass the duodenal obstruction by performing a duodenal jejunostomy. Abdominal insufflation is achieved with a varus needle in the left upper quadrant. The transverse colon is retracted cephalad, allowing identification of the ligament of Treitz. The peritoneum overlying the dilated duodenum, located to the right of the superior mesenteric artery, is identified and cleared.

The surgeon brings the jejunum into apposition with the duodenum, approximately thirty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. Stay sutures with 0 Surgidac suture are placed around the intended site of anastomosis. An anastomosis is created on the duodenal side using an ultrasonic scalpel, and a prestige grasper ensures intraluminal access. An anastomosis is also created on the jejunal side. An endo-GIA stapler with a 2.5 mm white load cartridge is introduced into the anastomosis and fired.

The surgeon encloses the common anastomosis using a 3-0 polysorb suture, carefully evaluating the integrity of both the suture line and the staple line. The trocars are then removed from the surgical site.
